name: Complete Bank
description: All 170 questions combined from every source

questions.question: Production data shows that in 12% of cases, your agent skips `get_customer` entirely and calls `lookup_order` using only the customer's stated name, occasionally leading to misidentified accounts and incorrect refunds. What change would most effectively address this reliability issue?

options.label: A
options.text: Add a programmatic prerequisite that blocks `lookup_order` and `process_refund` calls until `get_customer` has returned a verified customer ID
options.explanation: Programmatic enforcement provides deterministic guarantees. A PostToolUse hook or prerequisite gate physically blocks tools until verification completes. Zero% failure rate. CODE > PROMPTS.

options.label: B
options.text: Enhance the system prompt to state that customer verification via `get_customer` is mandatory before any order operations
options.explanation: Prompt instructions are probabilistic — the 12% skip rate exists despite the model generally knowing the right sequence. When errors have financial consequences, probabilistic compliance is unacceptable.

options.label: C
options.text: Add few-shot examples showing the agent always calling `get_customer` first, even when customers volunteer order details
options.explanation: Few-shot examples improve consistency but are still prompt-based guidance. They add token overhead and remain probabilistic. Same fundamental limitation as B.

options.label: D
options.text: Implement a routing classifier that analyzes each request and enables only the subset of tools appropriate for that request type
options.explanation: A routing classifier addresses which tools are available, not what order they must be called in. Solves tool selection, not tool sequencing — which is the actual problem.

questions.algorithmTrace: B and C are prompt-based → anti-pattern for deterministic business rules → eliminate. D solves wrong problem. A is programmatic enforcement → CODE > PROMPTS → select A.

questions.question: Production logs show the agent frequently calls `get_customer` when users ask about orders (e.g., "check my order #12345"), instead of calling `lookup_order`. Both tools have minimal descriptions and accept similar identifier formats. What's the most effective first step to improve tool selection reliability?

options.label: A
options.text: Add few-shot examples to the system prompt demonstrating correct tool selection patterns, with 5–8 examples
options.explanation: Few-shot examples add token overhead without fixing the root cause: the model can't distinguish between tools because descriptions are minimal. Descriptions are the primary selection signal.

options.label: B
options.text: Expand each tool's description to include input formats, example queries, edge cases, and boundaries explaining when to use it vs similar tools
options.explanation: Tool descriptions are the primary mechanism LLMs use for tool selection. Expanding them directly addresses the root cause. Low-effort, high-leverage fix. BUILT-IN > CUSTOM.

options.label: C
options.text: Implement a routing layer that parses user input and pre-selects the appropriate tool based on detected keywords
options.explanation: A keyword-based routing layer bypasses the LLM's natural language understanding and is fragile to edge cases. NL parsing for control flow anti-pattern.

options.label: D
options.text: Consolidate both tools into a single `lookup_entity` tool that accepts any identifier and internally determines which backend to query
options.explanation: Valid architectural choice but requires backend changes. The question asks for the most effective first step — description improvement is faster and often sufficient.

questions.algorithmTrace: C = NL parsing anti-pattern → eliminate. A adds overhead without fixing root cause. D is valid but heavy for a 'first step'. B is built-in mechanism → BUILT-IN > CUSTOM → select B.

questions.question: Your agent achieves 55% first-contact resolution, well below the 80% target. Logs show it escalates straightforward cases while attempting complex situations requiring policy exceptions. What's the most effective way to improve escalation calibration?

options.label: A
options.text: Add explicit escalation criteria to your system prompt with few-shot examples demonstrating when to escalate vs resolve autonomously
options.explanation: The root cause is unclear decision boundaries. Adding explicit criteria with few-shot examples directly fixes the calibration. EXPLICIT > IMPLICIT.

options.label: B
options.text: Have the agent self-report a confidence score (1–10) and automatically route requests to humans below a threshold
options.explanation: LLM self-reported confidence is poorly calibrated — the agent is already incorrectly confident on hard cases. A confidence score encodes the same bad calibration numerically.

options.label: C
options.text: Deploy a separate classifier model trained on historical tickets to predict which requests need escalation
options.explanation: Over-engineered, requiring labeled data and ML infrastructure when prompt optimization hasn't been tried. COST-AWARE > BRUTE-FORCE.

options.label: D
options.text: Implement sentiment analysis to detect customer frustration levels and automatically escalate when threshold exceeded
options.explanation: Sentiment-based escalation is an explicit exam anti-pattern. Sentiment doesn't correlate with case complexity — an angry customer with a standard replacement is still simple.

questions.algorithmTrace: B = self-reported confidence → unreliable → eliminate. D = sentiment-based escalation → anti-pattern → eliminate. C = over-engineered. A = explicit criteria → EXPLICIT > IMPLICIT → select A.

questions.question: You want to create a custom `/review` slash command that runs your team's standard code review checklist. This command should be available to every developer when they clone or pull the repository. Where should you create this command file?

options.label: A
options.text: In the `.claude/commands/` directory in the project repository
options.explanation: `.claude/commands/` is the project-scoped command directory. It's version-controlled and automatically available to all developers when they clone or pull the repo.

options.label: B
options.text: In `~/.claude/commands/` in each developer's home directory
options.explanation: `~/.claude/commands/` is for personal commands not shared via version control. Each developer would need to manually create the file.

options.label: C
options.text: In the `CLAUDE.md` file at the project root
options.explanation: CLAUDE.md is for project instructions and context, not command definitions.

options.label: D
options.text: In a `.claude/config.json` file with a `commands` array
options.explanation: `.claude/config.json` with a `commands` array is a fabricated configuration mechanism that does not exist in Claude Code. Trap answer.

questions.question: You've been assigned to restructure the team's monolithic application into microservices. This will involve changes across dozens of files and requires decisions about service boundaries. Which approach should you take?

options.label: A
options.text: Enter plan mode to explore the codebase, understand dependencies, and design an implementation approach before making changes
options.explanation: Plan mode is designed for complex tasks involving large-scale changes, multiple valid approaches, and architectural decisions — exactly what monolith restructuring requires.

options.label: B
options.text: Start with direct execution and make changes incrementally, letting the implementation reveal natural service boundaries
options.explanation: Starting with direct execution risks discovering dependencies late, leading to costly rework. Service boundaries require understanding the entire system first.

options.label: C
options.text: Use direct execution with comprehensive upfront instructions detailing exactly how each service should be structured
options.explanation: Assumes you already know the right structure without exploring the code. For a monolith, the right boundaries are unknown until you explore dependencies.

options.label: D
options.text: Begin in direct execution mode and only switch to plan mode if you encounter unexpected complexity
options.explanation: The complexity is already stated in the requirements — it's not something that 'might emerge later.' Ignoring obvious complexity wastes time.

questions.question: Your codebase has test files spread throughout (e.g., `Button.test.tsx` next to `Button.tsx`). You want all tests to follow the same conventions regardless of location. What's the most maintainable way to ensure Claude automatically applies correct conventions?

options.label: A
options.text: Create rule files in `.claude/rules/` with YAML frontmatter specifying glob patterns to conditionally apply conventions based on file paths
options.explanation: `.claude/rules/` with glob patterns (e.g., `paths: ["**/*.test.tsx"]`) allows conventions to be automatically applied regardless of directory location. Deterministic loading, zero manual invocation.

options.label: B
options.text: Consolidate all conventions in the root CLAUDE.md file under headers for each area, relying on Claude to infer which applies
options.explanation: Relies on Claude inferring which section applies — unreliable. Also wastes tokens loading all conventions when only some are relevant. EXPLICIT > IMPLICIT.

options.label: C
options.text: Create skills in `.claude/skills/` for each code type that include the relevant conventions
options.explanation: Skills require manual invocation, contradicting the need for deterministic 'automatic' application based on file paths.

options.label: D
options.text: Place a separate CLAUDE.md file in each subdirectory containing that area's specific conventions
options.explanation: Subdirectory CLAUDE.md files are directory-bound. If test files are spread across many directories, you'd need duplicate files everywhere — unmaintainable.

questions.question: After running the system on "impact of AI on creative industries," the final reports cover only visual arts, missing music, writing, and film. The coordinator's logs show it decomposed into: "AI in digital art creation," "AI in graphic design," and "AI in photography." What is the most likely root cause?

options.label: A
options.text: The synthesis agent lacks instructions for identifying coverage gaps
options.explanation: The synthesis agent can only synthesize what it receives. It received only visual arts findings because that's all subagents were assigned. Blames wrong agent.

options.label: B
options.text: The coordinator agent's task decomposition is too narrow, resulting in incomplete coverage
options.explanation: The coordinator's logs directly reveal the root cause: it decomposed 'creative industries' into only visual arts subtasks. Subagents executed correctly — the problem is what they were assigned.

options.label: C
options.text: The web search agent's queries are not comprehensive enough
options.explanation: The web search agent searched for what it was told to search for. Its queries were scoped by the coordinator's assignment. Can't blame the search for not searching unassigned topics.

options.label: D
options.text: The document analysis agent is filtering out non-visual creative industry sources
options.explanation: The document analysis agent was never given non-visual sources to analyze. It didn't filter anything — it simply worked within its assigned scope.

questions.question: The web search subagent times out while researching a complex topic. You need to design how this failure information flows back to the coordinator. Which error propagation approach best enables intelligent recovery?

options.label: A
options.text: Return structured error context including failure type, attempted query, partial results, and alternative approaches
options.explanation: Structured error context gives the coordinator all information needed for intelligent recovery — retry with modified query, try alternatives, or proceed with partial results.

options.label: B
options.text: Implement automatic retry with exponential backoff, returning generic "search unavailable" after all retries
options.explanation: Generic status hides valuable context from the coordinator, preventing informed recovery decisions.

options.label: C
options.text: Catch the timeout and return an empty result set marked as successful
options.explanation: Error suppression anti-pattern. The coordinator thinks the search found nothing when actually it failed. Prevents recovery; produces incomplete research unknowingly.

options.label: D
options.text: Propagate the timeout exception directly to terminate the entire workflow
options.explanation: Over-reaction. The coordinator could retry, delegate to another subagent, or proceed with partial results. Complete termination wastes all completed work.

questions.question: The synthesis agent frequently needs to verify claims, requiring 2–3 round trips through the coordinator to the web search agent (40% latency increase). 85% are simple fact-checks, 15% need deep investigation. What's the most effective approach?

options.label: A
options.text: Give the synthesis agent a scoped `verify_fact` tool for simple lookups, while complex verifications continue through the coordinator
options.explanation: Applies principle of least privilege: gives synthesis only what it needs for the 85% common case while preserving the coordination pattern for complex cases.

options.label: B
options.text: Have synthesis accumulate all verification needs and batch them to the coordinator at the end
options.explanation: Batching creates blocking dependencies — synthesis steps may depend on earlier verified facts. Can't synthesize paragraph 3 if paragraph 1's fact-check hasn't returned.

options.label: C
options.text: Give synthesis access to all web search tools for any verification directly
options.explanation: Over-provisions the synthesis agent, violating separation of concerns and least privilege. Now it's also a search agent. Potentially >5 tools anti-pattern.

options.label: D
options.text: Have the web search agent proactively cache extra context anticipating verification needs
options.explanation: Speculative caching cannot reliably predict what synthesis will need to verify. Over-fetches in most cases while still missing some verification needs.

questions.question: Your pipeline script runs `claude "Analyze this pull request for security issues"` but the job hangs indefinitely. Logs indicate Claude Code is waiting for interactive input. What's the correct approach?

options.label: A
options.text: Add the `-p` flag: `claude -p "Analyze this pull request for security issues"`
options.explanation: The `-p` (or `--print`) flag is the documented way to run Claude Code in non-interactive mode. It processes the prompt, outputs to stdout, and exits.

options.label: B
options.text: Set the environment variable `CLAUDE_HEADLESS=true` before running the command
options.explanation: `CLAUDE_HEADLESS=true` is a fabricated environment variable that does not exist in Claude Code. Trap answer.

options.label: C
options.text: Redirect stdin from `/dev/null`: `claude "..." < /dev/null`
options.explanation: A Unix workaround that doesn't properly address Claude Code's command syntax. Not the designed non-interactive mode.

options.label: D
options.text: Add the `--batch` flag: `claude --batch "..."`
options.explanation: `--batch` is a fabricated flag that does not exist in Claude Code. The real flag is `-p` / `--print`.

questions.question: Your team has two workflows: (1) blocking pre-merge check and (2) overnight technical debt report. Your manager proposes switching both to the Message Batches API for 50% cost savings. How should you evaluate this?

options.label: A
options.text: Use batch processing for the technical debt reports only; keep real-time calls for pre-merge checks
options.explanation: Message Batches API has up to 24-hour processing with no latency SLA. Unsuitable for blocking pre-merge checks but ideal for overnight batch jobs. COST-AWARE > BRUTE-FORCE.

options.label: B
options.text: Switch both workflows to batch processing with status polling
options.explanation: Polling doesn't solve the fundamental problem: batch processing has no guaranteed completion time. Developers could be blocked for hours.

options.label: C
options.text: Keep real-time calls for both workflows to avoid batch result ordering issues
options.explanation: Wastes the 50% savings opportunity on overnight reports with zero latency requirements. Batch results can be correlated using `custom_id` fields.

options.label: D
options.text: Switch both to batch with a timeout fallback to real-time if batches take too long
options.explanation: Over-engineered. The simpler solution is matching each API to its appropriate use case. No fallback logic needed.

questions.question: A PR modifying 14 files produces inconsistent review results: detailed feedback for some files, superficial for others, contradictory findings flagging a pattern as bad in one file but approving identical code elsewhere. How should you restructure?

options.label: A
options.text: Split into focused passes: analyze each file individually for local issues, then run a separate integration pass examining cross-file data flow
options.explanation: Splitting reviews into focused passes directly addresses attention dilution. File-by-file analysis ensures consistent depth; a separate integration pass catches cross-file issues.

options.label: B
options.text: Require developers to split large PRs into smaller submissions of 3–4 files
options.explanation: Shifts burden to developers without improving the system. Large PRs are sometimes necessary.

options.label: C
options.text: Switch to a higher-tier model with a larger context window
options.explanation: Larger context windows don't solve attention quality issues. The problem isn't capacity — it's attention distribution.

options.label: D
options.text: Run three independent review passes on the full PR and only flag issues appearing in at least two
options.explanation: Requiring consensus suppresses real bugs caught intermittently. Reduces sensitivity without addressing root cause.

questions.question: You are implementing an agentic loop. After each API response, your loop needs to decide whether to continue or stop. Which approach correctly implements loop control flow?

options.label: A
options.text: Check if `stop_reason` is `"tool_use"` to continue, terminate when `stop_reason` is `"end_turn"`
options.explanation: The `stop_reason` field is the designed mechanism. `"tool_use"` → continue loop, `"end_turn"` → terminate. Deterministic and reliable.

options.label: B
options.text: Parse the assistant's text for phrases like "I'm done" or "task complete"
options.explanation: NL parsing for control flow is an explicit anti-pattern. The model might use "done" mid-task. `stop_reason` exists to replace this.

options.label: C
options.text: Set a maximum iteration count of 10 and stop when reached
options.explanation: Arbitrary iteration caps as the primary stopping mechanism is an anti-pattern. Task might need 12 steps or only 2. Caps should be safety guardrails, not primary control.

options.label: D
options.text: Check if the response contains text content (vs only tool calls) as the termination signal
options.explanation: The model can return text alongside tool calls (explaining actions), or tool calls without text. Text presence is not a reliable proxy for task completion.

questions.question: Your multi-agent system uses hub-and-spoke. Subagent B sometimes refers to findings from subagent A, even though you never passed those findings. What's happening?

options.label: A
options.text: Subagents automatically inherit the coordinator's full conversation history
options.explanation: Subagents operate with isolated context — they do NOT inherit the coordinator's history automatically. Key exam concept.

options.label: B
options.text: The subagents share a memory store that synchronizes findings in real-time
options.explanation: No real-time memory sharing exists between subagents. Each has isolated context.

options.label: C
options.text: The coordinator is accidentally including subagent A's results when constructing subagent B's prompt
options.explanation: Since subagent context must be explicitly provided in the prompt, the most likely explanation is the coordinator's prompt construction accidentally includes A's results.

options.label: D
options.text: Claude caches subagent context across the session, making all findings available
options.explanation: Claude does not cache subagent context across sessions or make findings automatically available to other subagents.

questions.question: Your coordinator needs to invoke three subagents simultaneously. What is the correct way to achieve parallel subagent execution?

options.label: A
options.text: Use Python threading to call the Claude API three times concurrently
options.explanation: While threading works mechanically, it's not the Agent SDK's designed pattern for parallel subagent execution.

options.label: B
options.text: Have the coordinator emit multiple `Task` tool calls in a single response
options.explanation: The Agent SDK supports parallel execution by emitting multiple `Task` tool calls in one coordinator response. Coordinator's `allowedTools` must include `"Task"`.

options.label: C
options.text: Configure `parallel_execution: true` in the agent definition
options.explanation: `parallel_execution: true` is a fabricated option. Trap answer.

options.label: D
options.text: Create three separate coordinator instances, each managing one subagent
options.explanation: Three coordinators duplicate orchestration logic and break the hub-and-spoke pattern.

questions.question: Company policy requires refunds over $500 must be approved by a human supervisor. The system prompt instruction is bypassed ~3% of the time. What's the most reliable fix?

options.label: A
options.text: Add three few-shot examples demonstrating the $500 threshold behavior
options.explanation: Still probabilistic. Moving from 3% to 1% failure is still unacceptable for unauthorized financial transactions.

options.label: B
options.text: Implement a hook that intercepts `process_refund` tool calls and blocks any refund above $500, redirecting to human approval
options.explanation: A programmatic hook provides deterministic enforcement. Every call is intercepted and checked. 0% bypass rate. CODE > PROMPTS for financial rules.

options.label: C
options.text: Bold and capitalize the rule: "**CRITICAL: NEVER process refunds > $500**"
options.explanation: Formatting emphasis has marginal effect on compliance rates. The model already 'knows' the rule — the issue is probabilistic adherence.

options.label: D
options.text: Add a self-check step where the agent reviews the refund amount before processing
options.explanation: Same-session self-review anti-pattern. The agent retains the same reasoning context and is unlikely to contradict its initial decision.

questions.question: You need to review a large PR (15 files) for both per-file code quality AND cross-file architectural consistency. Which decomposition pattern is most appropriate?

options.label: A
options.text: Prompt chaining: analyze each file individually, then run a cross-file integration pass
options.explanation: Prompt chaining is ideal for predictable multi-aspect reviews. Per-file local analysis ensures consistent depth; separate cross-file pass catches integration issues.

options.label: B
options.text: Dynamic decomposition: let the agent decide which files to review based on initial findings
options.explanation: Dynamic decomposition is for open-ended investigation where scope isn't known upfront. Here, you know exactly what files need review.

options.label: C
options.text: Single-pass: provide all 15 files in one prompt with instructions for both checks
options.explanation: Processing 15 files simultaneously causes attention dilution — inconsistent depth, missed bugs, contradictory feedback.

options.label: D
options.text: Three-stage pipeline: quality checker → architecture checker → integration checker per file
options.explanation: Over-engineered sequential bottleneck. Architecture check can't work per-file — it needs cross-file context.

questions.question: During a lengthy codebase investigation, Claude starts referencing "typical patterns" rather than specific classes it discovered earlier. What should you do?

options.label: A
options.text: Continue — the model has all the information in its context
options.explanation: Referencing 'typical patterns' instead of specifics is a context degradation signal. Continuing produces increasingly unreliable results.

options.label: B
options.text: Use `/compact` to reduce context usage, then continue
options.explanation: `/compact` reduces context by summarizing verbose discovery output while preserving key findings. Directly addresses context degradation during extended exploration.

options.label: C
options.text: Start a fresh session and re-discover everything
options.explanation: Starting fresh discards all accumulated findings. Re-discovery wastes significant time. Better to compact and continue.

options.label: D
options.text: Use `--resume` to restore a checkpoint from earlier
options.explanation: `--resume` continues the same session — it doesn't reduce context. Degraded context remains degraded.

questions.question: Your financial agent must: (1) verify identity, (2) check account status, (3) process transaction. Which enforcement ensures this sequence is always followed?

options.label: A
options.text: Include a "MANDATORY WORKFLOW" section in the system prompt
options.explanation: Prompt instructions are probabilistic for business-critical sequences. In financial services, even 1% deviation is unacceptable.

options.label: B
options.text: Implement prerequisite hooks that block each step until the previous completes successfully
options.explanation: Prerequisite hooks provide deterministic enforcement. Physically impossible to skip steps. CODE > PROMPTS for critical workflows.

options.label: C
options.text: Use `tool_choice` forced selection to force specific tools on specific turns
options.explanation: Assumes the agent always takes exactly 3 turns in order. Real conversations are dynamic. Forced selection is for single-step guarantees, not multi-step workflows.

options.label: D
options.text: Provide 5 few-shot examples showing the correct workflow sequence
options.explanation: Few-shot examples improve consistency but cannot guarantee the full sequence. Still prompt-based.

questions.question: Your agent calls MCP tools returning timestamps in different formats: Unix epoch, ISO 8601, and US date strings. The model struggles to compare dates. Best approach?

options.label: A
options.text: Add system prompt instructions explaining each tool's format
options.explanation: Telling the model about formats doesn't make it better at comparing '1719849600' with '07/01/2024'. Still has to mentally convert.

options.label: B
options.text: Implement `PostToolUse` hooks that normalize all timestamps to a consistent format
options.explanation: `PostToolUse` hooks intercept tool results and transform data before the model processes it. Normalizing all timestamps means the model receives directly comparable dates.

options.label: C
options.text: Create a `convert_timestamp` tool the model can call
options.explanation: Adds unnecessary tool calls and relies on the model recognizing when conversion is needed. Programmatic normalization is more efficient.

options.label: D
options.text: Update tool descriptions to specify the expected timestamp format
options.explanation: Better descriptions help understanding but don't help actually comparing different format timestamps.

questions.question: You're evaluating two refactoring strategies — Strategy A (extract service) vs Strategy B (extract library). You want to explore both without cross-contamination. Best approach?

options.label: A
options.text: Explore both in the same session, asking Claude to "forget" the previous analysis
options.explanation: Models can't 'forget' — previous analysis is still in context and will influence reasoning about the second strategy.

options.label: B
options.text: Use `fork_session` to create two independent branches from the current analysis baseline
options.explanation: `fork_session` creates independent branches from a shared baseline. Both forks start with the same understanding but explore divergent approaches without contamination.

options.label: C
options.text: Open two separate Claude Code terminals and start fresh in each
options.explanation: Starting fresh means re-discovering the codebase twice. Fork preserves the shared baseline.

options.label: D
options.text: Explore Strategy A, then use `/compact` and explore Strategy B
options.explanation: `/compact` reduces context but doesn't eliminate it. Strategy A's analysis will still influence Strategy B. Residual contamination.

questions.question: You need to investigate intermittent production failures. Initial error logs suggest network issues, but you're not sure of the full scope. Which decomposition pattern?

options.label: A
options.text: Prompt chaining: network logs → application logs → database logs → synthesize
options.explanation: Too rigid for open-ended investigation. The issue might be in deployment pipeline (not on the checklist). Fixed sequences miss unexpected root causes.

options.label: B
options.text: Dynamic adaptive decomposition: generate initial plan, adjust based on discoveries
options.explanation: Dynamic decomposition generates subtasks based on what is discovered, not a predetermined sequence. If network logs reveal DNS, the next step investigates DNS specifically.

options.label: C
options.text: Single comprehensive prompt covering all possible failure modes
options.explanation: Trying to check everything produces shallow investigation rather than deep analysis where it matters.

options.label: D
options.text: Parallel decomposition: simultaneously investigate network, application, and database layers
options.explanation: Investigating all layers simultaneously when logs suggest network issues wastes resources on unlikely paths.

questions.question: Coordinator invokes subagent C after A and B complete. Subagent C needs findings from both. How should you provide this context?

options.label: A
options.text: Include A and B's complete findings directly in subagent C's prompt
options.explanation: Subagent context must be explicitly provided in the prompt. Including findings directly ensures C has everything it needs. Documented approach.

options.label: B
options.text: Store findings in a shared database and give subagent C access credentials
options.explanation: Over-engineered. Direct prompt inclusion is simpler and more reliable for passing findings.

options.label: C
options.text: Pass subagent A and B's agent IDs so C can query them directly
options.explanation: Violates hub-and-spoke — subagents shouldn't communicate directly. All inter-agent communication routes through the coordinator.

options.label: D
options.text: Set `inherit_context: true` in subagent C's configuration
options.explanation: `inherit_context: true` is fabricated. Subagents do NOT automatically inherit parent context.

questions.question: Your coordinator always invokes all 4 subagents even when only 2 are relevant. How should you improve it?

options.label: A
options.text: Hard-code keyword-based routing rules mapping queries to subagent combinations
options.explanation: Keyword routing is brittle and misses nuanced queries. NL parsing anti-pattern.

options.label: B
options.text: Design the coordinator to analyze query requirements and dynamically select which subagents to invoke
options.explanation: Coordinators should dynamically select subagents based on query analysis rather than always routing through the full pipeline. Documented best practice.

options.label: C
options.text: Reduce to 2 subagents by merging pairs
options.explanation: Merging reduces specialization and increases tool count per agent. The problem is routing, not agent count.

options.label: D
options.text: Add a separate classifier before the coordinator
options.explanation: The coordinator IS the classification layer. Adding a separate one is redundant and adds latency.

questions.question: Your agent has `analyze_content`, `analyze_document`, and `review_document` — three similar tools frequently misrouted. What's the best improvement?

options.label: A
options.text: Rename tools to more distinct names
options.explanation: Names help but tool descriptions are the primary selection signal. If descriptions remain vague, misrouting continues.

options.label: B
options.text: Add a system prompt section listing when to use each tool
options.explanation: System prompt instructions for tool selection are secondary to tool descriptions.

options.label: C
options.text: Split into purpose-specific tools with clear descriptions, input/output contracts, and explicit boundary statements
options.explanation: Clear descriptions with boundaries ('Use extract_metadata for X, NOT for Y') directly address misrouting root cause.

options.label: D
options.text: Reduce to one unified `analyze` tool handling all cases
options.explanation: Super-tool hides routing decision inside the tool's implementation. Makes scope vague.

questions.question: Your MCP tool encounters a network timeout. It currently returns `{"status": "error", "message": "Operation failed"}`. What's the best improvement?

options.label: A
options.text: Return structured error: `{isError: true, errorCategory: "transient", isRetryable: true, attempted: "...", alternatives: [...]}`
options.explanation: Structured error responses with isError, errorCategory, isRetryable enable intelligent recovery decisions. Documented MCP pattern.

options.label: B
options.text: Silently return an empty result set to avoid interrupting the agent
options.explanation: Error suppression anti-pattern. Agent can't distinguish 'API timed out' from 'no data found.' Prevents recovery.

options.label: C
options.text: Throw an unhandled exception that terminates the current turn
options.explanation: Over-reaction. The agent should receive error context and decide how to proceed.

options.label: D
options.text: Return `{"status": "error", "message": "Network timeout. Please try again later."}`
options.explanation: Human-readable but lacks structured metadata for programmatic recovery. Agent can't easily determine retryability.

questions.question: Your single agent has 18 tools covering customer management, orders, shipping, billing, analytics, and reporting. Tool selection accuracy is 60%. Best architectural change?

options.label: A
options.text: Improve all 18 tool descriptions
options.explanation: 18 tools is simply too many regardless of description quality. Decision complexity exceeds reliable discrimination capacity.

options.label: B
options.text: Split into specialized subagents with 4–5 tools each, coordinated by a hub agent
options.explanation: Restricting each subagent to 4-5 relevant tools dramatically reduces decision complexity. Addresses the >5 tools anti-pattern.

options.label: C
options.text: Implement a keyword-based routing layer
options.explanation: NL parsing anti-pattern. Fragile, doesn't reduce per-agent tool count.

options.label: D
options.text: Use `tool_choice` forced selection to always pick the most likely tool
options.explanation: Forced selection eliminates the model's ability to choose the right tool. Would always call the same tool regardless of context.

questions.question: You're setting up a team Jira MCP server needing `JIRA_API_TOKEN`. How should you configure this?

options.label: A
options.text: Hard-code the token in `.mcp.json` and add to `.gitignore`
options.explanation: Hard-coding tokens is a security anti-pattern. Files can be accidentally committed.

options.label: B
options.text: Configure in `.mcp.json` with `${JIRA_API_TOKEN}` environment variable expansion
options.explanation: `.mcp.json` supports environment variable expansion. Config is version-controlled, actual tokens stay in the environment. Secure credential management.

options.label: C
options.text: Store the token in `~/.claude.json` under MCP server configuration
options.explanation: `~/.claude.json` is personal. Each developer would configure individually; not automatic for new team members.

options.label: D
options.text: Create a team-shared API token and distribute via secure messaging
options.explanation: Shared tokens via messaging is insecure. Individual tokens with env var expansion is more secure.

questions.question: Your extraction pipeline must always extract metadata first. The agent has `extract_metadata`, `analyze_content`, and `generate_summary`. How do you ensure metadata extraction happens first?

options.label: A
options.text: Set `tool_choice: "auto"` with a system prompt instruction
options.explanation: `auto` allows the model to return text or call a different tool. Prompt instructions are probabilistic.

options.label: B
options.text: Set `tool_choice: {"type": "tool", "name": "extract_metadata"}` for the first API call
options.explanation: Forced tool selection guarantees the model calls `extract_metadata` specifically. The designed mechanism for ensuring a specific tool is called first.

options.label: C
options.text: Set `tool_choice: "any"` to force a tool call
options.explanation: `any` forces some tool call but doesn't control which one. Model might call `generate_summary` instead.

options.label: D
options.text: Remove other tools from the first call's tool list
options.explanation: Works as a workaround, but with `auto` the model might return text instead of calling the tool. Forced selection is more reliable.

questions.question: A developer needs to find all React test files importing a specific testing utility in a large monorepo. Which built-in tool combination?

options.label: A
options.text: Glob to find `**/*.test.tsx`, then Read each file
options.explanation: Reading every test file to check imports is wasteful. There could be hundreds. Grep is designed for content search.

options.label: B
options.text: Grep across the entire codebase for the import statement
options.explanation: Too broad — might match the import in non-test files. Scoping to test files first is more precise.

options.label: C
options.text: Glob for `**/*.test.tsx`, then Grep within those files for the import pattern
options.explanation: Two-step: Glob for file matching → Grep for content search. Each built-in tool used for its designed purpose. Efficient and precise.

options.label: D
options.text: Bash to run `find` and `grep` commands
options.explanation: Built-in tools (Glob, Grep) are preferred over Bash equivalents. BUILT-IN > CUSTOM.

questions.question: Your MCP server has 10,000+ issues. Agents make many exploratory calls to find relevant ones. How can you reduce this overhead?

options.label: A
options.text: Add a comprehensive `search_issues` tool
options.explanation: A search tool helps but the agent still needs calls to discover what's available. Multiple queries needed.

options.label: B
options.text: Expose issue summaries as MCP resources that the agent can browse as a content catalog
options.explanation: MCP resources expose content catalogs reducing exploratory tool calls. The agent browses the catalog before making targeted calls. Designed purpose of MCP resources.

options.label: C
options.text: Cache the last 100 issues in the system prompt
options.explanation: Wastes tokens on irrelevant issues, becomes stale quickly. Not scalable.

options.label: D
options.text: Limit the agent to issues from the last 7 days
options.explanation: Arbitrary restriction. May exclude relevant older issues. Doesn't address the fundamental exploratory overhead.

questions.question: Agent queries a customer database and receives `{"results": [], "count": 0}`. It interprets this as failure and retries 3 times. What's the design issue?

options.label: A
options.text: The tool should return an error instead of empty results
options.explanation: An empty result IS a valid successful response. Returning an error for a successful query with no matches would be misleading.

options.label: B
options.text: The tool's response doesn't distinguish between 'successful query with no matches' and 'query failed'
options.explanation: The fundamental issue is ambiguity. The tool should clearly indicate success vs failure. The agent needs to distinguish 'nothing found' from 'couldn't look.'

options.label: C
options.text: The agent needs better retry logic with exponential backoff
options.explanation: Better retry logic doesn't fix the ambiguity. The agent would still retry successful queries.

options.label: D
options.text: The agent's system prompt should explain that empty results are valid
options.explanation: Prompt instructions are fallback, not primary fix. Fix the tool, not the prompt.

questions.question: Your team needs Jira integration. An open-source Jira MCP server exists with 500+ GitHub stars. A developer proposes building custom. Best approach?

options.label: A
options.text: Always build custom for a perfect workflow fit
options.explanation: Over-engineering. 'Perfect fit' rarely justifies the maintenance burden.

options.label: B
options.text: Use the existing community MCP server, extending only if specific features are missing
options.explanation: Choosing existing community MCP servers over custom implementations for standard integrations is documented best practice. BUILT-IN > CUSTOM.

options.label: C
options.text: Build custom but reference the community server's code
options.explanation: Still creates a maintenance fork. Better to use the original and contribute upstream.

options.label: D
options.text: Use both — community for basic, custom for advanced
options.explanation: Two servers for the same service creates confusion and double maintenance.

questions.question: A new developer clones the repo and reports Claude doesn't follow coding standards. Others confirm it works for them. Standards are in `~/.claude/CLAUDE.md` on the original dev's machine. Root cause?

options.label: A
options.text: The new developer needs to install a Claude Code extension
options.explanation: Configuration doesn't require special extensions. The issue is scope.

options.label: B
options.text: The coding standards are in user-level config (`~/.claude/CLAUDE.md`), not shared via version control — they should be project-level
options.explanation: `~/.claude/CLAUDE.md` is user-level, not version-controlled. Team standards should be in project-level config (`.claude/CLAUDE.md` or root `CLAUDE.md`).

options.label: C
options.text: The new developer needs to run `/memory` to load team config
options.explanation: `/memory` shows which memory files are loaded but doesn't transfer another user's personal configuration.

options.label: D
options.text: Claude Code configuration is cached and requires a restart
options.explanation: Fabricated technical detail. Claude Code reads configuration files on each session.

questions.question: Your project has Terraform in `terraform/` and Python in `src/`. You want Terraform conventions to load ONLY when editing Terraform files. Best configuration?

options.label: A
options.text: Create `terraform/CLAUDE.md` with the conventions
options.explanation: Works for that directory but `.claude/rules/` with globs is more maintainable and supports patterns spanning multiple directories.

options.label: B
options.text: Create `.claude/rules/terraform.md` with YAML frontmatter `paths: ["terraform/**/*"]`
options.explanation: `.claude/rules/` with path scoping loads rules only when editing matching files. Reduces irrelevant context and token usage. Designed mechanism.

options.label: C
options.text: Add Terraform conventions to root CLAUDE.md under a header
options.explanation: Root CLAUDE.md loads for every file, wasting tokens on Terraform conventions when editing Python. EXPLICIT > IMPLICIT.

options.label: D
options.text: Create a skill in `.claude/skills/terraform/`
options.explanation: Skills require manual invocation — wrong for always-on conventions that should load automatically.

questions.question: You want a codebase analysis skill that produces verbose output. You're concerned it will clutter the main conversation. What frontmatter configuration?

options.label: A
options.text: `verbose: false` to suppress output
options.explanation: Not a valid skill frontmatter option. Trap answer.

options.label: B
options.text: `context: fork` to run in an isolated sub-agent context
options.explanation: `context: fork` runs skills in isolation, preventing verbose output from polluting the main conversation. Only the summary returns. Designed for this use case.

options.label: C
options.text: `max_tokens: 500` to limit output length
options.explanation: Not a valid skill frontmatter option. Token limits are API parameters.

options.label: D
options.text: `output: summary` to automatically summarize results
options.explanation: Fabricated option. The correct way to isolate verbose output is `context: fork`.

questions.question: You've created a personal `/quick-test` command using your preferred testing style. Teammates have different preferences. Where should you create it?

options.label: A
options.text: `.claude/commands/quick-test.md` in the project repository
options.explanation: `.claude/commands/` is version-controlled and shared. Your personal preferences would affect everyone.

options.label: B
options.text: `~/.claude/commands/quick-test.md` in your home directory
options.explanation: `~/.claude/commands/` is user-scoped. Personal commands not shared via version control.

options.label: C
options.text: `.claude/skills/quick-test/` with `personal: true`
options.explanation: Skills aren't slash commands, and `personal: true` is not valid frontmatter.

options.label: D
options.text: Add it to your user-level `~/.claude/CLAUDE.md`
options.explanation: CLAUDE.md is for instructions and context, not command definitions.

questions.question: A developer has a stack trace: null pointer exception at `UserService.java` line 45. Clear missing null check. Which approach?

options.label: A
options.text: Enter plan mode to analyze codebase architecture
options.explanation: Over-kill for a clear, well-scoped bug. Plan mode is for complex tasks with multiple valid approaches.

options.label: B
options.text: Use direct execution to add the null check at line 45
options.explanation: Direct execution for simple, well-scoped changes with clear scope. No ambiguity about what to do or where.

options.label: C
options.text: Use plan mode to explore all callers of the method
options.explanation: Unnecessary exploration for a simple fix. Over-investigation.

options.label: D
options.text: Start plan mode, switch to direct execution after confirming scope is small
options.explanation: Scope is already stated as clear. Unnecessary overhead.

questions.question: Claude generates dates as "MM/DD/YYYY" sometimes and "YYYY-MM-DD" other times despite instructions saying "use ISO format." Prose instructions haven't helped. Best fix?

options.label: A
options.text: Rephrase more clearly: "Always format dates as YYYY-MM-DD"
options.explanation: Already tried prose instructions — they produce inconsistent results. Same approach, same result.

options.label: B
options.text: Provide 2–3 concrete input/output examples showing the expected ISO format
options.explanation: Concrete input/output examples are the most effective way to communicate expected transformations when prose descriptions produce inconsistent results.

options.label: C
options.text: Add a post-processing step to reformat dates
options.explanation: Treats the symptom, not the cause. Adds complexity without improving model behavior.

options.label: D
options.text: Switch to a stronger model
options.explanation: The issue is instruction clarity, not model capability. A stronger model with the same ambiguous instructions may produce the same inconsistency.

questions.question: Your CI uses Claude to review PRs. The same session that generated code is reviewing it. Developers report it almost never finds issues. What should change?

options.label: A
options.text: Add a more critical review prompt
options.explanation: Prompt emphasis doesn't overcome same-session bias. The model retains its own reasoning context.

options.label: B
options.text: Use a separate, independent Claude instance without the generator's reasoning context
options.explanation: Same-session self-review is an explicit anti-pattern. An independent review instance without prior reasoning context is more effective. ISOLATED > SHARED.

options.label: C
options.text: Increase temperature for variability
options.explanation: Temperature affects randomness, not critical evaluation capability.

options.label: D
options.text: Add a 5-second delay between generation and review
options.explanation: Time delays don't reset context. Same biased context persists regardless of delays.

questions.question: Your CI pipeline needs Claude to output findings as machine-parseable JSON for automated inline PR comments. Which CLI flags?

options.label: A
options.text: `-p` only, then parse text output with regex
options.explanation: Parsing free-text with regex is fragile and breaks when output format varies.

options.label: B
options.text: `-p` with `--output-format json` and `--json-schema`
options.explanation: These flags produce guaranteed machine-parseable structured output. `-p` for non-interactive, `--output-format json` for JSON, `--json-schema` for schema enforcement.

options.label: C
options.text: `-p` with `--format json`
options.explanation: `--format json` is not a valid Claude Code CLI flag. The correct flag is `--output-format json`. Trap.

options.label: D
options.text: `-p` with a system prompt saying "respond in JSON format"
options.explanation: Prompt-based 'respond in JSON' doesn't guarantee valid JSON or schema compliance.

questions.question: Your root CLAUDE.md has grown to 500 lines. It's hard to maintain. Best approach to reorganize?

options.label: A
options.text: Split into topic-specific files in `.claude/rules/` (e.g., `testing.md`, `api-conventions.md`)
options.explanation: Documented approach for managing large configs. Each file can have path-scoping. Supports `@import` for cross-referencing.

options.label: B
options.text: Keep single CLAUDE.md with better headers and table of contents
options.explanation: Better organization helps humans but doesn't reduce token cost of loading 500 lines into every context.

options.label: C
options.text: Move everything to subdirectory CLAUDE.md files
options.explanation: Subdirectory files are for directory-specific conventions, not topic-specific rules spanning many directories.

options.label: D
options.text: Convert to a structured JSON configuration file
options.explanation: Claude Code uses CLAUDE.md markdown files, not JSON configs.

questions.question: Your skill requires a file path input. Developers keep invoking it without the argument, causing errors. What frontmatter option?

options.label: A
options.text: `required: true`
options.explanation: Not a valid skill frontmatter option.

options.label: B
options.text: `argument-hint: "path/to/file"`
options.explanation: `argument-hint` prompts developers for required parameters when they invoke the skill without arguments. Shows expected input format.

options.label: C
options.text: `validate-args: true`
options.explanation: Not a valid skill frontmatter option.

options.label: D
options.text: `default-arg: "."`
options.explanation: Not a valid skill frontmatter option.

questions.question: Your code review bot has too many false positives. Adding "be conservative" and "only report high-confidence findings" didn't help. Best fix?

options.label: A
options.text: Lower temperature to reduce creative findings
options.explanation: Temperature doesn't control the distinction between bugs and style issues.

options.label: B
options.text: Define explicit categorical criteria: report bugs/security, skip minor style and speculative concerns
options.explanation: General instructions like 'be conservative' fail without boundaries. Explicit categorical criteria give clear rules for include vs exclude. EXPLICIT > IMPLICIT.

options.label: C
options.text: Use extended thinking for more careful reasoning
options.explanation: Doesn't address the criteria gap. The model thinks more carefully but still doesn't know which categories to report.

options.label: D
options.text: Train a separate classifier to filter false positives
options.explanation: Over-engineered. Fix the prompt first — explicit criteria address root cause with zero infrastructure. COST-AWARE > BRUTE-FORCE.

questions.question: Your invoice extraction tool occasionally fabricates line items for non-standard invoice formats. Best improvement?

options.label: A
options.text: Add validation checking if totals match line item sums
options.explanation: Catches only arithmetic mismatches. A fabricated line item with fabricated amount can still sum correctly.

options.label: B
options.text: Provide 2–4 few-shot examples showing extraction from varied formats, including handling of missing/unclear info
options.explanation: Few-shot examples showing how to handle ambiguity (returning null for unclear fields) directly address hallucination. The model learns 'when uncertain, leave blank.'

options.label: C
options.text: Increase max_tokens so the model doesn't truncate
options.explanation: Truncation isn't the issue — fabrication is. The model isn't running out of space.

options.label: D
options.text: Switch to a specialized OCR model
options.explanation: OCR handles text recognition from images. The problem is extraction logic during structured extraction, not OCR.

questions.question: You need guaranteed schema-compliant JSON output from Claude. Which approach provides the strongest guarantee?

options.label: A
options.text: System prompt: "Always respond in valid JSON matching this schema"
options.explanation: Prompt instructions don't guarantee valid JSON. Model might include explanatory text or markdown fences.

options.label: B
options.text: Use `tool_use` with a JSON schema definition and `tool_choice: "any"`
options.explanation: `tool_use` with schemas eliminates JSON syntax errors by design. `tool_choice: "any"` guarantees structured output rather than free text. Strongest guarantee.

options.label: C
options.text: Use `tool_use` with a JSON schema and post-validate with `strict: true`
options.explanation: `strict: true` eliminates syntax errors at the API level — separate post-validation isn't needed for syntax.

options.label: D
options.text: Parse text response with a JSON parser and retry on syntax errors
options.explanation: Retry-based approach adds latency and doesn't guarantee semantic correctness. `tool_use` solves this more elegantly.

questions.question: You're extracting invoice data. Some invoices have PO numbers, some don't. How should you design the `po_number` field?

options.label: A
options.text: Required string field with default "N/A"
options.explanation: Required fields pressure the model to produce values. Downstream must check for 'N/A' as special case.

options.label: B
options.text: Optional (nullable) field that allows null when not found in the document
options.explanation: Nullable fields allow null for genuinely absent information, preventing fabrication. Cleaner for downstream processing.

options.label: C
options.text: Required field with instructions to "leave blank if not found"
options.explanation: 'Leave blank' is ambiguous — empty string? null? omitted? Schema-level nullability is more precise.

options.label: D
options.text: Two separate schemas — one with PO, one without
options.explanation: Over-engineered. Doubles the config surface. A single schema with nullable field handles both cases.

questions.question: Extraction pipeline validates data. After 3 retries, extraction still fails. Source document has a genuine arithmetic error (printed total is wrong). Conclusion?

options.label: A
options.text: The model needs more retries with error feedback
options.explanation: The source data is wrong. No retries can extract a correct total that doesn't exist.

options.label: B
options.text: The model is not powerful enough
options.explanation: Model correctly extracted what's there — the document itself is wrong. Not a capability issue.

options.label: C
options.text: Retries are ineffective when source data is wrong — needs human review routing
options.explanation: Retries fix format/structural errors, not source data errors. This requires human review — the system should flag it rather than retrying indefinitely.

options.label: D
options.text: Loosen the validation to allow small discrepancies
options.explanation: Loosening validation might hide real extraction errors. The validation caught a genuine document issue.

questions.question: A 20-file code review produces inconsistent findings — same pattern flagged in file 3 but approved in file 15. What architectural pattern?

options.label: A
options.text: Use a stronger model
options.explanation: Doesn't address attention dilution. Even stronger models suffer from this at 20 files.

options.label: B
options.text: Run review 5 times and take majority vote
options.explanation: Majority voting suppresses intermittent real findings. Reduces sensitivity.

options.label: C
options.text: Multi-pass: per-file local analysis then a cross-file consistency pass
options.explanation: Per-file ensures consistent depth. Cross-file pass checks for consistency. Addresses both attention dilution and cross-file inconsistency.

options.label: D
options.text: Sort files alphabetically before processing
options.explanation: File ordering doesn't address attention dilution. Cosmetic change.

questions.question: Which statement about the Message Batches API is correct?

options.label: A
options.text: It supports multi-turn tool calling within a single batch request
options.explanation: Batch API does NOT support multi-turn tool calling. Each request is single-turn.

options.label: B
options.text: It guarantees completion within 1 hour for batches under 100 requests
options.explanation: No guaranteed latency SLA. Processing can take up to 24 hours.

options.label: C
options.text: It offers 50% cost savings with up to 24-hour processing and uses `custom_id` for correlation
options.explanation: Correct: 50% savings, up to 24h, `custom_id` for request/response correlation.

options.label: D
options.text: It automatically retries failed requests
options.explanation: No automatic retries. You must handle failures by resubmitting failed documents via `custom_id`.

questions.question: Developers frequently dismiss your review bot's findings. You want to analyze which categories are most dismissed. What to add?

options.label: A
options.text: A confidence score (1-10) per finding
options.explanation: Model confidence is poorly calibrated — often confident about false positives.

options.label: B
options.text: A `detected_pattern` field to enable analysis of false positive patterns
options.explanation: `detected_pattern` (e.g., 'unused-import', 'missing-null-check') enables systematic analysis of which patterns are dismissed, allowing targeted precision improvements.

options.label: C
options.text: A severity rating (low/medium/high)
options.explanation: Helps prioritize but doesn't enable analysis of which types are dismissed.

options.label: D
options.text: A reference link to the coding standard
options.explanation: Helps developers understand why flagged but doesn't enable aggregate dismiss pattern analysis.

questions.question: After 15+ turns, your support agent loses track of order numbers, refund amounts, and delivery dates. Progressive summarization condenses these into vague summaries. Fix?

options.label: A
options.text: Increase context window to hold all messages
options.explanation: Larger window delays but doesn't fix detail loss during summarization. Eventually fills up.

options.label: B
options.text: Extract transactional facts into a persistent "case facts" block that is never summarized
options.explanation: A persistent case facts block is extracted once and included in every prompt, outside summarized history. Key facts never condensed.

options.label: C
options.text: Use a database to store all conversation details
options.explanation: Over-engineered. Case facts block achieves the same with zero infrastructure.

options.label: D
options.text: Force resolution within 5 messages
options.explanation: Arbitrary constraint that doesn't improve retention. Complex issues need many turns.

questions.question: Your research agent processes 20 sources but reliably cites only the first 3 and last 3, omitting middle sources. Mitigation?

options.label: A
options.text: Reduce to 6 sources
options.explanation: Loses coverage. Goal is to use all 20 effectively.

options.label: B
options.text: Place key findings summary at the beginning with explicit section headers and priority ordering
options.explanation: 'Lost in the middle' effect: models process beginning/end positions more reliably. Key findings summary at the beginning surfaces critical info at a high-attention position.

options.label: C
options.text: Randomly shuffle source order each time
options.explanation: Randomizes which sources are omitted — non-deterministic behavior. Doesn't solve the problem.

options.label: D
options.text: Process sources one at a time
options.explanation: Prevents cross-source synthesis. Extremely slow for 20 sources.

questions.question: Which is a legitimate escalation trigger in a customer support agent?

options.label: A
options.text: Customer uses profanity
options.explanation: Profanity reflects frustration, not case complexity. An angry customer with a simple issue should still be resolvable.

options.label: B
options.text: Agent's self-reported confidence drops below 6/10
options.explanation: Self-reported confidence is poorly calibrated. Not a valid trigger.

options.label: C
options.text: Customer explicitly requests to speak with a human agent
options.explanation: One of three documented legitimate triggers (along with policy gaps and inability to make progress). Should be honored immediately.

options.label: D
options.text: Sentiment analysis detects "very negative" tone
options.explanation: Sentiment-based escalation is an explicit anti-pattern. Negative tone ≠ needs human.

questions.question: Your `get_order_details` returns 40+ fields but only 5 are relevant. Tool results accumulate over multiple calls. What to do?

options.label: A
options.text: Increase context window to accommodate verbose responses
options.explanation: 87.5% of each response is irrelevant noise consuming tokens. Larger window just tolerates waste.

options.label: B
options.text: Trim verbose tool outputs to only relevant fields before they accumulate
options.explanation: Trimming before context entry prevents bloat. Each call saves 35 fields worth of tokens, adding up over multiple calls.

options.label: C
options.text: Tell the model to "ignore irrelevant fields"
options.explanation: Even ignored, fields still consume context tokens. Trimming prevents the consumption.

options.label: D
options.text: Create a new filtered tool returning only 5 fields
options.explanation: Requires backend changes, not always feasible for third-party tools. Trimming in orchestration is more universal.

questions.question: Deep codebase investigation over many turns — agent starts contradicting earlier findings. What technique helps?

options.label: A
options.text: Periodically restart and re-discover
options.explanation: Loses accumulated context. Re-discovery wastes time.

options.label: B
options.text: Maintain scratchpad files recording key findings, referencing them for subsequent analysis
options.explanation: Scratchpad files persist findings outside the context window, providing a stable source of truth. Counteracts context degradation.

options.label: C
options.text: Increase temperature for more creative exploration
options.explanation: Higher temperature increases randomness, making contradictions MORE likely.

options.label: D
options.text: Use `--resume` to save/restore session periodically
options.explanation: `--resume` continues the same degraded context. Doesn't reduce or create external persistence.

questions.question: Your extraction system achieves 97% overall accuracy. Manager wants to reduce human review to 10%. What analysis should you do first?

options.label: A
options.text: Run 100 more test documents to confirm the rate
options.explanation: More of the same aggregate metric. 97% could mask 99.5% common + 60% rare.

options.label: B
options.text: Analyze accuracy by document type and field to verify consistent performance across all segments
options.explanation: Aggregate metrics may mask poor performance on specific types/fields. Stratified analysis reveals if all segments perform equally.

options.label: C
options.text: Check if 97% was on the same distribution
options.explanation: Important but insufficient. Doesn't reveal per-segment accuracy.

options.label: D
options.text: Survey human reviewers about their confidence
options.explanation: Subjective, not data-driven. Doesn't substitute for quantitative analysis.

questions.question: Your multi-agent research system combines findings. Two subagents found conflicting GDP figures from credible sources. What should the synthesis output do?

options.label: A
options.text: Average the two conflicting figures
options.explanation: Averaging assumes both are equally valid and truth is in between. GDP methodology differences make averaging meaningless.

options.label: B
options.text: Pick the figure from the more recent source
options.explanation: Recency doesn't guarantee accuracy. Silently picking one hides the conflict.

options.label: C
options.text: Annotate both figures with source attribution and let the coordinator or end user decide
options.explanation: Preserve both values with source attribution and annotate the conflict explicitly. Preserves information provenance.

options.label: D
options.text: Omit the conflicting statistic entirely
options.explanation: Information loss. Conflicting data from credible sources is important information, not noise.

questions.question: Your coordinator dispatches 3 subagents in parallel. Two return in 2 seconds, but the third handles a complex sub-task taking 30+ seconds. The coordinator waits for all results. Best optimization?

options.label: A
options.text: Set a global timeout and kill slow subagents
options.explanation: Arbitrary timeouts may kill legitimate work. The 30-second task may be correctly processing a complex request.

options.label: B
options.text: Process completed results immediately while waiting; synthesize incrementally as results arrive
options.explanation: Incremental synthesis processes early results immediately, reducing end-to-end latency. The final result includes the slow subagent's output when it arrives.

options.label: C
options.text: Run all 3 subagents sequentially to avoid parallel complexity
options.explanation: Sequential execution increases total time to 34+ seconds vs 30 seconds in parallel. Going backward.

options.label: D
options.text: Give the slow subagent more tools to work faster
options.explanation: More tools don't make an inherently complex task faster. May worsen tool selection accuracy.

questions.question: You're building a document processing pipeline. Step 1 extracts text, Step 2 classifies the document, Step 3 extracts structured data based on classification. Which orchestration pattern?

options.label: A
options.text: Prompt chaining — each step feeds into the next with defined inputs/outputs
options.explanation: Prompt chaining is ideal when steps are sequential, deterministic, and each output feeds the next input. This pipeline has clear dependencies.

options.label: B
options.text: Hub-and-spoke with a coordinator delegating to extraction, classification, and structuring subagents
options.explanation: Hub-and-spoke is for tasks that can be parallelized. Here, Step 3 depends on Step 2 which depends on Step 1 — inherently sequential.

options.label: C
options.text: Dynamic adaptive decomposition based on document content
options.explanation: The pipeline is predictable. Dynamic decomposition is for open-ended tasks where scope is unknown upfront.

options.label: D
options.text: Single large prompt with all three instructions
options.explanation: Combining extraction, classification, and structured extraction in one prompt creates attention dilution and blurs responsibilities.

questions.question: Your agent processes customer requests. Some require tool calls, others can be answered from context alone. After each API response, which check should come FIRST?

options.label: A
options.text: Check if any text content was returned
options.explanation: Text content can accompany tool calls (explanations) or appear in final responses. Not diagnostic.

options.label: B
options.text: Check the `stop_reason` field to determine if the model wants to call a tool or has finished
options.explanation: `stop_reason` is the documented first check: `tool_use` → execute tool and loop; `end_turn` → return to user. It's the primary loop control signal.

options.label: C
options.text: Check the token count to see if context is exhausted
options.explanation: Token count is a resource metric, not a loop control signal. Checking stop_reason handles max_tokens too.

options.label: D
options.text: Parse the response for action keywords like 'I need to' or 'Let me'
options.explanation: NL parsing for control flow is an explicit anti-pattern. The structured stop_reason field exists for this.

questions.question: Your multi-agent system needs an audit trail showing which subagent produced each piece of the final output. Best approach?

options.label: A
options.text: Log subagent names in the coordinator's internal notes
options.explanation: Internal notes may be summarized or lost over long conversations. Not a reliable audit trail.

options.label: B
options.text: Have each subagent self-identify in its output text
options.explanation: Self-identification in NL text is inconsistent and hard to parse programmatically.

options.label: C
options.text: Implement provenance tracking in the coordinator that tags each result with its source subagent before synthesis
options.explanation: Programmatic provenance tracking at the coordinator level provides reliable, structured attribution for every piece of the final output.

options.label: D
options.text: Use different Claude models for different subagents so outputs are distinguishable
options.explanation: Model selection for identification purposes is wasteful and unreliable. Models don't produce distinctly identifiable output.

questions.question: Your agent's agentic loop occasionally enters an infinite cycle — calling the same tool with the same parameters repeatedly. Best safeguard?

options.label: A
options.text: Set max_tokens very low to force early termination
options.explanation: Low max_tokens truncates output but doesn't prevent re-invocation on the next turn. Loop continues.

options.label: B
options.text: Add a maximum iteration count as a safety guardrail, plus detection for repeated identical tool calls
options.explanation: Iteration caps as guardrails (not primary control) combined with duplicate call detection catches degenerate loops. Primary control is still stop_reason.

options.label: C
options.text: Parse text for 'I'm stuck' to detect loops
options.explanation: NL parsing anti-pattern. The model doesn't reliably announce that it's stuck.

options.label: D
options.text: Increase temperature to add randomness
options.explanation: Randomness might break the exact pattern but doesn't reliably prevent loops and may cause other issues.

questions.question: Your MCP server provides both `get_user(id)` and `search_users(query)`. The agent frequently calls `get_user` with a name string instead of an ID. Root cause?

options.label: A
options.text: The agent is hallucinating IDs
options.explanation: The agent isn't making up IDs — it's passing a name TO the ID parameter. The issue is tool descriptions, not hallucination.

options.label: B
options.text: Tool descriptions don't clearly explain when to use each tool and what input format each expects
options.explanation: Insufficient descriptions cause misrouting. `get_user` should say 'Requires numeric user ID. For name/email lookup, use search_users instead.' Boundaries prevent misuse.

options.label: C
options.text: The parameter name 'id' is confusing — rename to 'user_id'
options.explanation: Renaming helps marginally but descriptions are the primary signal. Without boundary descriptions, the same confusion recurs.

options.label: D
options.text: Remove `get_user` and only use `search_users`
options.explanation: Eliminates the direct lookup which is more efficient when you have the ID. Throwing away functionality isn't ideal.

questions.question: Your tool returns timestamps as Unix epoch and prices as raw cents (e.g., 1599 for $15.99). The agent struggles to present these to users. Best solution?

options.label: A
options.text: Add formatting instructions to the system prompt
options.explanation: Prompt instructions for data transformation are probabilistic. Math conversion in prompts is error-prone.

options.label: B
options.text: Have the tool return pre-formatted human-readable strings
options.explanation: Pre-formatting removes the ability to do calculations or comparisons on raw values.

options.label: C
options.text: Return both raw values and human-readable formatted values in tool responses
options.explanation: Including both formats lets the model use raw values for calculations and formatted values for presentation. Best of both worlds.

options.label: D
options.text: Create a separate `format_value` tool
options.explanation: Adds unnecessary tool calls for every value. Including formatted values in the original response is more efficient.

questions.question: Your MCP server exposes a `delete_account` tool. In production, an agent accidentally deletes a customer account during a routine inquiry. What safety mechanism was missing?

options.label: A
options.text: The tool description should have said 'use with caution'
options.explanation: 'Use with caution' is a prompt instruction, not a safety mechanism. Probabilistic compliance for destructive operations is unacceptable.

options.label: B
options.text: A human-in-the-loop approval gate for destructive operations
options.explanation: Destructive operations (delete, modify financial data) must require explicit human approval before execution. This is a PostToolUse hook gating pattern.

options.label: C
options.text: Lower temperature to reduce unexpected behavior
options.explanation: Temperature doesn't prevent tool misuse. The model decided to call the tool — temperature affects randomness, not tool selection logic.

options.label: D
options.text: Remove the delete tool entirely
options.explanation: May be needed for legitimate operations. The fix is gating, not removal.

questions.question: Your tool schema has a `status` field defined as `enum: ["active", "inactive", "pending"]`. The model occasionally returns `"Active"` (capitalized) or `"ACTIVE"`. Best fix?

options.label: A
options.text: Add prompt instructions: 'Use lowercase for status values'
options.explanation: Prompt-based enforcement for data formatting is probabilistic.

options.label: B
options.text: Add a validation-retry loop that rejects non-matching values and asks the model to correct
options.explanation: Validation against the schema catches incorrect casing. The retry with specific error feedback teaches the model the exact format. Reliable enforcement.

options.label: C
options.text: Normalize casing in a post-processing step
options.explanation: Post-processing works but treats the symptom. Validation-retry teaches the model, improving future accuracy.

options.label: D
options.text: Expand the enum to include all casing variations
options.explanation: Permissive schemas don't solve the problem — they push complexity to downstream consumers.

questions.question: Your team wants to expose a company wiki as an MCP integration. The wiki has 50,000 pages. The agent needs to find relevant pages before reading them. Best MCP pattern?

options.label: A
options.text: Expose all pages as MCP resources
options.explanation: 50,000 resources would overwhelm any resource listing. Not practical at scale.

options.label: B
options.text: Create a `search_wiki` tool and a `read_page` tool, using resource templates for page URIs
options.explanation: Search tool for discovery, read tool for content, and resource URI templates (e.g., `wiki://pages/{page_id}`) for addressing. Standard MCP pattern for large data sources.

options.label: C
options.text: Load top 100 pages into the system prompt
options.explanation: Wastes tokens on potentially irrelevant pages. Doesn't scale and becomes stale.

options.label: D
options.text: Expose one tool that searches and returns full page content in one call
options.explanation: Returning full content from a search is wasteful — search results should be summaries with the option to read full content.

questions.question: Which MCP primitive should you use to provide Claude with a pre-written code review template that includes placeholders?

options.label: A
options.text: MCP Tool — define a code-review tool
options.explanation: Tools execute actions. A template is static content, not an action to execute.

options.label: B
options.text: MCP Resource — expose the template as a readable resource
options.explanation: Resources expose data/content, but templates with placeholders that guide Claude's behavior are prompts, not resources.

options.label: C
options.text: MCP Prompt — expose the template as a parameterized prompt
options.explanation: MCP Prompts are specifically designed for reusable prompt templates with parameters. A code review template with placeholders maps directly to this primitive.

options.label: D
options.text: MCP Sampling — use server-initiated model invocation
options.explanation: Sampling lets the server ask Claude to generate content. A template doesn't need server-initiated generation.

questions.question: Your team's CLAUDE.md says 'Use TypeScript strict mode.' A subdirectory `legacy/` contains old JavaScript code that breaks with strict TypeScript rules. Best configuration?

options.label: A
options.text: Add an exception comment in root CLAUDE.md: '(except legacy/ directory)'
options.explanation: This relies on the model reading and remembering a parenthetical exception. Not deterministic.

options.label: B
options.text: Create `legacy/CLAUDE.md` with JavaScript-specific conventions
options.explanation: Subdirectory CLAUDE.md adds context but still loads the root CLAUDE.md's TypeScript rules alongside.

options.label: C
